---
title: "Who Gets the Credit?"
subtitle: "Understanding Marketing Attribution Models"
author: "Corrine"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    fontsize: 1.8em
    margin: 0.08
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## The Problem {background-color="#1E2761"}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.2em; margin-top: 1em;"}
A customer sees a **Facebook ad** →
searches on **Google** →
clicks an **email** →
**buys**.
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1.5em; font-size: 1.1em; color: #CADCFC;"}
Who gets the credit for that conversion?
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; font-size: 0.9em; color: #aaa;"}
The answer depends entirely on which attribution model you choose.
:::

---

## 5 Attribution Models {background-color="#1C2951"}

| Model | Logic |
|---|---|
| **First-touch** | 100% credit → first touchpoint |
| **Last-touch** | 100% credit → last touchpoint |
| **Linear** | Credit split equally across all touches |
| **Time Decay** | More credit to touches closer to conversion |
| **Position-based** | 40% first · 20% middle · 40% last |

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em; color: #CADCFC; margin-top: 1em;"}
Same customer journey. Five different answers.
:::

---

## Visualizing the Difference {background-color="#1C2951"}

*One journey: Facebook → Google → Email → Purchase*

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

models = ['First-touch', 'Last-touch', 'Linear', 'Time Decay', 'Position-based']
channels = ['Facebook', 'Google', 'Email']

# Credit allocation per model
data = {
    'First-touch':     [1.00, 0.00, 0.00],
    'Last-touch':      [0.00, 0.00, 1.00],
    'Linear':          [0.33, 0.33, 0.33],
    'Time Decay':      [0.14, 0.29, 0.57],
    'Position-based':  [0.40, 0.20, 0.40],
}

x = np.arange(len(channels))
width = 0.15
colors = ['#4FC3F7', '#81C784', '#FFD54F', '#FF8A65', '#CE93D8']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#1C2951')
ax.set_facecolor('#1C2951')

for i, (model, credits) in enumerate(data.items()):
    offset = (i - 2) * width
    bars = ax.bar(x + offset, credits, width, label=model, color=colors[i], alpha=0.9)

ax.set_ylabel('Credit Allocated', color='white', fontsize=12)
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(channels, color='white', fontsize=13)
ax.tick_params(colors='white')
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.1)
ax.yaxis.set_tick_params(labelcolor='white')
ax.spines[:].set_color('#555')
ax.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=9, framealpha=0.3, labelcolor='white')
ax.set_title('Credit Allocation by Model — Same Journey', color='white', fontsize=13, pad=12)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('attribution_chart.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#1C2951')
plt.show()
```

---

## The Numbers Can Diverge Drastically {background-color="#1C2951"}

*Imagine 1,000 conversions across Facebook, Google, Email*

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Simulated channel-level credit (sum across all journeys)
models = ['First-touch', 'Last-touch', 'Linear', 'Time Decay', 'Position-based']
facebook = [420, 50,  280, 160, 370]
google   = [130, 120, 280, 260, 190]
email    = [50,  530, 280, 380, 240]
# remaining goes to "Other"
other    = [1000-f-g-e for f,g,e in zip(facebook,google,email)]

x = np.arange(len(models))
width = 0.55
colors_ch = ['#4FC3F7', '#81C784', '#FFD54F', '#CE93D8']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#1C2951')
ax.set_facecolor('#1C2951')

b1 = ax.bar(x, facebook, width, label='Facebook', color=colors_ch[0])
b2 = ax.bar(x, google,   width, bottom=facebook, label='Google', color=colors_ch[1])
b3 = ax.bar(x, email,    width, bottom=[f+g for f,g in zip(facebook,google)], label='Email', color=colors_ch[2])

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(models, color='white', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Attributed Conversions (out of 1,000)', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax.tick_params(colors='white')
ax.yaxis.set_tick_params(labelcolor='white')
ax.spines[:].set_color('#555')
ax.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=10, framealpha=0.3, labelcolor='white')
ax.set_title('Same 1,000 Conversions — Very Different Channel Credit', color='white', fontsize=13, pad=12)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('channel_chart.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#1C2951')
plt.show()
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em; color: #FFD54F; margin-top: 0.5em;"}
Facebook credit: **50 → 420** depending on model chosen.
:::

---

## Which Model Should You Use? {background-color="#1C2951"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Business Goal → Model**

- 🎯 Brand awareness → **First-touch**
- 💰 Direct conversion → **Last-touch**
- ⚖️ Balanced view → **Linear**
- 🕐 Recency matters → **Time Decay**
- 🔁 Nurture + close → **Position-based**
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Advanced: Spend-Weighted**

Weight credit not just by position,
but by **how much was spent**
at each touchpoint.

::: {style="color: #CADCFC; font-size: 0.85em; margin-top: 0.8em;"}
Channels that cost more
get held to a higher standard.
:::
:::
:::

---

## Takeaway {background-color="#1E2761"}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.3em; margin-top: 1.5em; line-height: 1.6;"}
Attribution isn't a statistics problem.

It's a **business decision**.
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 0.95em; color: #CADCFC; margin-top: 1.5em;"}
The model you choose determines which channels
get budget next quarter.

Choose deliberately.
:::
